title: World Council of Churches

infobox.key: [Principal symbol of Christianity](/wiki/Christian_cross "Principal symbol of Christianity")
infobox.value: | * [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") * [Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")  * [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") * [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") * [Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") * [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") * [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") * [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus")

(**WCC**) is a worldwide [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") inter-church organization founded in 1948 to work for the cause of [ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism").[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1) Its full members today include the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), most jurisdictions of the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church")[[2]](#cite_note-2) (including the [Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) the [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), the [Union of Utrecht](/wiki/Union_of_Utrecht_(Old_Catholic) "Union of Utrecht (Old Catholic)"), the [Lutheran World Federation](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation"), the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), the [Mennonite churches](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), the [World Methodist Council](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council"), the [Baptist World Alliance](/wiki/Baptist_World_Alliance "Baptist World Alliance"), the [World Communion of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Communion_of_Reformed_Churches "World Communion of Reformed Churches"), the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), the [Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Mar_Thoma_Syrian_Church "Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church"), and several [Pentecostal churches](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") (but not the [Pentecostal World Fellowship](/wiki/Pentecostal_World_Fellowship "Pentecostal World Fellowship") or the [Assemblies of God](/wiki/Assemblies_of_God "Assemblies of God")).[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1)[[4]](#cite_note-Roberson1995-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") is not a full member, but it sends delegates who have observer status to meetings.[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The **World Council of Churches** (**WCC**) is a worldwide [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") inter-church organization founded in 1948 to work for the cause of [ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism").[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1) Its full members today include the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), most jurisdictions of the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church")[[2]](#cite_note-2) (including the [Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople")),[[3]](#cite_note-3) the [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), the [Union of Utrecht](/wiki/Union_of_Utrecht_(Old_Catholic) "Union of Utrecht (Old Catholic)"), the [Lutheran World Federation](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation"), the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), the [Mennonite churches](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), the [World Methodist Council](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council"), the [Baptist World Alliance](/wiki/Baptist_World_Alliance "Baptist World Alliance"), the [World Communion of Reformed Churches](/wiki/World_Communion_of_Reformed_Churches "World Communion of Reformed Churches"), the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), the [Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Mar_Thoma_Syrian_Church "Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church"), and several [Pentecostal churches](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") (but not the [Pentecostal World Fellowship](/wiki/Pentecostal_World_Fellowship "Pentecostal World Fellowship") or the [Assemblies of God](/wiki/Assemblies_of_God "Assemblies of God")).[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1)[[4]](#cite_note-Roberson1995-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") is not a full member, but it sends delegates who have observer status to meetings.[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The WCC describes itself as "a worldwide fellowship of 352 global, regional and sub-regional, national and local churches seeking unity, a common witness and Christian service".[[7]](#cite_note-7) It has no head office as such, but its administrative centre is at the [Ecumenical Centre](/wiki/Ecumenical_Centre "Ecumenical Centre") in [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The organization's members include [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") which claim to collectively represent over 600 million people across the world in more than 110 countries.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-k650-10)

Many regional affiliates of the World Council of Churches, such as the [Middle East Council of Churches](/wiki/Middle_East_Council_of_Churches "Middle East Council of Churches") and the [National Council of Churches in Australia](/wiki/National_Council_of_Churches_in_Australia "National Council of Churches in Australia"), work for the cause of Christian unity at the domestic level, with member denominations including the Oriental Orthodox churches, Lutheran churches, Catholic Church, Eastern Orthodox churches, Methodist churches, Anglican Communion, and Reformed churches among others.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The [Ecumenical Movement](/wiki/Christian_ecumenism "Christian ecumenism") met with initial successes in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, including the [Edinburgh Missionary Conference](/wiki/Edinburgh_Missionary_Conference "Edinburgh Missionary Conference") of 1910 (chaired by future WCC honorary president [John R. Mott](/wiki/John_R._Mott "John R. Mott")). In 1920, the former [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople"), [Germanus V](/wiki/Germanus_V_of_Constantinople "Germanus V of Constantinople"), wrote a letter addressed 'To all the Churches of Christ, wherever they may be', urging closer co-operation among separated Christians, and suggesting a 'League of Churches', parallel to the newly founded [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")".[[13]](#cite_note-Ware1993-13) Church leaders agreed in 1937 to establish a World Council of Churches, based on a merger of the *[Faith and Order Movement](/wiki/Faith_and_Order_Movement "Faith and Order Movement")* (under [Charles Brent](/wiki/Charles_Brent "Charles Brent") of the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") of the United States) and *[Life and Work Movement](/wiki/Life_and_Work_(conference) "Life and Work (conference)")* (under [Nathan Söderblom](/wiki/Nathan_S%C3%B6derblom "Nathan Söderblom") of the Lutheran [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden")) organisations.

Its official establishment was deferred with the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") until 23 August 1948. Delegates of 147 churches assembled in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") to merge the [Faith and Order Movement](/wiki/Faith_and_Order_Movement "Faith and Order Movement") and Life and Work Movement.[[14]](#cite_note-14) This was consolidated by a second meeting at [Lund](/wiki/Lund "Lund") in 1950, for which the British Methodist [Robert Newton Flew](/wiki/Robert_Newton_Flew "Robert Newton Flew") edited an influential volume of studies, *The Nature of the Church*.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Subsequent mergers were with the [International Missionary Council](/wiki/International_Missionary_Council "International Missionary Council") in 1961 and the World Council of Christian Education, with its roots in the 18th century [Sunday School](/wiki/Sunday_School "Sunday School") movement, in 1971.

WCC member churches include the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East") and the [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Church "Oriental Orthodox Church"), almost all of the [Eastern Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church"); the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"); the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"); some [Old Catholic Churches](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church"); the Methodist churches; the Baptists churches; the [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") and other [Reformed churches](/wiki/Reformed_churches "Reformed churches"), a sampling of [united](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches") and independent churches, and some [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") churches.[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1)[[4]](#cite_note-Roberson1995-4)

Many churches who refused to join the WCC joined to form the [World Evangelical Alliance](/wiki/World_Evangelical_Alliance "World Evangelical Alliance").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Neither the WCC nor the U.S.-based [National Council of Churches](/wiki/National_Council_of_Churches "National Council of Churches") (NCC) include denominations such as [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") ([Mormons](/wiki/Mormons "Mormons")), and other groups which have historically refused to affirm certain widely accepted creeds of Christendom (for example, the [Holy Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") as canonized by the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed")).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Delegates sent from the member churches meet every seven or eight years in an Assembly, which elects a Central Committee that governs between Assemblies. A variety of other committees and commissions answer to the Central Committee and its staff. Assemblies have been held since 1948.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/1951_Greek_special_passport_for_Italy_%26_Switzerland_and_used_to_participate_in_the_work_of_the_%22World_Council_of_Churches%22.jpg/250px-1951_Greek_special_passport_for_Italy_%26_Switzerland_and_used_to_participate_in_the_work_of_the_%22World_Council_of_Churches%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:1951_Greek_special_passport_for_Italy_%26_Switzerland_and_used_to_participate_in_the_work_of_the_%22World_Council_of_Churches%22.jpg)

1951 Greek special passport for Italy & Switzerland and used to participate in the work of the "World Council of Churches"

The "human rights abuses in communist countries evoked grave concern among the leaders of the World Council of Churches."[[18]](#cite_note-18) However, historian Christopher Andrew claims that, during the Cold War, a number of important WCC representatives of the Orthodox Church in Eastern Europe had been working for the KGB, and that they influenced the policy of the WCC.[[19]](#cite_note-andrew-19) From 1955 to 1958, [Robert S. Bilheimer](/wiki/Robert_S._Bilheimer "Robert S. Bilheimer") co-chaired a WCC international commission to prepare a document addressing the threat of [nuclear warfare](/wiki/Nuclear_warfare "Nuclear warfare") during the Cold War.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

At the 1961 conference, a 32-year-old Russian Orthodox Bishop named Aleksey Ridiger was sent as delegate to the assembly, and then appointed to the WCC's central committee. He was later elected as Russian patriarch in 1990 as [Alexei II](/wiki/Alexy_II_of_Moscow "Alexy II of Moscow").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The ninth assembly took place in [Porto Alegre, Brazil](/wiki/Porto_Alegre,_Brazil "Porto Alegre, Brazil") in February 2006, under the theme "God, in your grace, transform the world".[[22]](#cite_note-22) During the first Assemblies, theologians [Vasileios Ioannidis](/wiki/Vasileios_Ioannidis "Vasileios Ioannidis") and Amilkas Alivizatos contributed significantly to the debates that led to the drafting of the "Toronto Statement", a foundational document which facilitated Eastern Orthodox participation in the organization and today it constitutes its ecclesiological charter.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The 10th Assembly was held in [Busan](/wiki/Busan "Busan"), Republic of Korea, from 30 October to 8 November 2013.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 2013 Dr. [Agnes Abuom](/wiki/Agnes_Abuom "Agnes Abuom") of Nairobi, from the Anglican Church of Kenya, was elected as moderator of the Central Committee of the World Council of Churches; she is the first woman and the first African to hold this position.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

The 11th Assembly of the World Council of Churches took place in [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe"), Germany, from 31 August to 8 September 2022, under the theme "Christ's love moves the world to reconciliation and unity".[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Events and presidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Events and presidents")]

### Assemblies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Assemblies")]

The World Council of Churches has held 11 Assemblies to date, starting with the founding assembly in 1948:[[28]](#cite_note-28)

1. [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), 22 August – 4 September 1948
2. [Evanston](/wiki/Evanston,_Illinois "Evanston, Illinois"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), United States, 15–31 August 1954
3. [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), 19 November – 5 December 1961
4. [Uppsala](/wiki/Uppsala "Uppsala"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), 4–20 July 1968
5. [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), 23 November – 10 December 1975
6. [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver,_British_Columbia "Vancouver, British Columbia"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") 24 July – 10 August 1983

## Commissions and teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Commissions and teams")]

There are two complementary approaches to ecumenism: dialogue and action. The *Faith and Order Movement* and *Life and Work Movement* represent these approaches.[[38]](#cite_note-38) These approaches are reflected in the work of the WCC in its [commissions](http://www.wcc-coe.org/wcc/who/commissions.html), these being:

* Echos – Commission on Youth (ages 18–30)
* Commission of the Churches on Diakonia and Development
* Commission on Education and Ecumenical Formation
* Commission of the Churches on International Affairs
* Commission on Justice, Peace and Creation
* Commission on World Mission and Evangelism
* Faith and Order Plenary Commission and the Faith and Order Standing Commission
* Joint Consultative Group with Pentecostals
* Joint Working Group WCC – Catholic Church (Vatican)
* Reference Group on the Decade to Overcome Violence
* Reference Group on Inter-Religious Relations
* Special Commission on Orthodox Participation in the WCC

### Diakonia and development and international relations commissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Diakonia and development and international relations commissions")]

The WCC acts through both its member churches and other religious and social organizations to coordinate ecumenical, evangelical, and social action.

Current WCC programs include a [Decade to Overcome Violence](/wiki/Decade_to_Overcome_Violence "Decade to Overcome Violence"), an international campaign to combat [AIDS/HIV](/wiki/AIDS "AIDS") in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and the *Justice, Peace and Integrity of Creation (JPIC)* initiative.

### Faith and Order Commission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Faith and Order Commission")]

Main article: [Faith and Order Commission](/wiki/Faith_and_Order_Commission "Faith and Order Commission")

WCC's [Faith and Order Commission](/wiki/Faith_and_Order_Commission "Faith and Order Commission") has been successful in working toward consensus on [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"), and [Ministry](/wiki/Christian_ministry "Christian ministry"), on the date of [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), on the nature and purpose of the [church](/wiki/Christian_church "Christian church") ([ecclesiology](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology")), and on ecumenical [hermeneutics](/wiki/Hermeneutics "Hermeneutics").

#### Texts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Texts")]

* *[Baptism, Eucharist and Ministry](/wiki/Baptism,_Eucharist_and_Ministry "Baptism, Eucharist and Ministry")* (Faith and Order Paper No. 111, the "Lima Text"; 1982)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Justice, Peace and Creation Commission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Justice, Peace and Creation Commission")]

Justice, Peace and Integrity of Creation (JPIC) has drawn many elements together with an [environmental](/wiki/Environment_(biophysical) "Environment (biophysical)") focus. Its mandate is:

> To analyze and reflect on justice, peace and creation *in their interrelatedness*, to promote values and practices that make for a [culture of peace](/wiki/Culture_of_Peace "Culture of Peace"), and to work towards a culture of solidarity with young people, women, Indigenous Peoples and racially and ethnically oppressed people.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Focal issues have been [globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization") and the emergence of [new social movements](/wiki/New_social_movements "New social movements") (in terms of people *bonding together* in the struggle for justice, peace, and the protection of creation).[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Attention has been given to issues around:

* economy[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* environment[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* Indigenous Peoples[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* peace[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* people with disabilities[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* [racism](/wiki/Programme_to_Combat_Racism "Programme to Combat Racism")[[51]](#cite_note-51)
* women[[52]](#cite_note-52)
* youth[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Relations with the Roman Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Relations with the Roman Catholic Church")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/World_Council_of_Churches "Special:EditPage/World Council of Churches") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(March 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The largest Christian body, the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"), is not a member of the WCC, but has worked closely with the council for more than three decades and sends observers to all major WCC conferences as well as to its Central Committee meetings and the Assemblies (cf. [Joint Working Group](/w/index.php?title=Joint_Working_Group_between_the_Catholic_Church_and_the_World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joint Working Group between the Catholic Church and the World Council of Churches (page does not exist)")).

The [Pontifical Council for Promoting Christian Unity](/wiki/Pontifical_Council_for_Promoting_Christian_Unity "Pontifical Council for Promoting Christian Unity") also nominates 12 members to the WCC's *Faith and Order Commission* as full members. While not a member of the WCC, the Catholic Church is a member of some other ecumenical bodies at regional and national levels, for example, the [National Council of Churches in Australia](/wiki/National_Council_of_Churches_in_Australia "National Council of Churches in Australia") and the National Council of Christian Churches in Brazil (CONIC).

[Pope Pius XI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XI "Pope Pius XI") stated in 1928, that the only means by which the world Christian community was to return to faith, was to return to Roman Catholic worship. In this regard, the Papacy rejected, to a great extent, the idea of the participation of the Catholic Church within the World Council of Churches.

Pius XI stated that the ‘One True Church’ was that of the Roman Catholic denomination, and therefore there was the implication that the Catholic Church was not permitted at this stage to engage with other denominations, which the Papacy considered to be irrelevant. A similar policy was followed by his successor, [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII"); the Catholic Church, therefore, did not attend the 1948 meeting of the WCC, in addition to the idea that all members of the Church were barred from attending WCC conferences.

[Pope John XXIII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXIII "Pope John XXIII") took a different stance however, and in 1958 he was elected as the head of the Catholic Church. [Ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") was a new element of Catholic ideology which had been permitted, which was signified to a great extent, when John XXIII met with the then Archbishop of Canterbury, [Geoffrey Fisher](/wiki/Geoffrey_Fisher "Geoffrey Fisher"). This was the first meeting between an Archbishop of Canterbury, and the Pope in the Vatican for 600 years. John XXIII later developed the office of the [Secretariat for Promoting Christian Unity](/wiki/Pontifical_Council_for_Promoting_Christian_Unity "Pontifical Council for Promoting Christian Unity"); which symbolised a dramatic shift in support for the ecumenical movement, from the Catholic Church, led from the Vatican. 1961 saw Catholic members attend the Delhi conference of the WCC, which marked a significant shift in attitude toward the WCC from the Papacy. There was the idea in addition to this, that the Pope invited non-Catholics to attend the Vatican II Council.[[1]](#cite_note-Harmon2010-1) This new approach to inter-denominational relations was marked within the *[Unitatis Redintegratio](/wiki/Unitatis_redintegratio "Unitatis redintegratio")* decree.

This document marked several key reforms within the Catholic approach:

I. ‘[Separated brethren](/wiki/Separated_brethren "Separated brethren")’ was the new term for non-Catholics, as opposed to the previously used ‘heretics’ [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[54]](#cite_note-54)

II. Both Catholic and non-Catholic elements are held responsible for [the schism between Catholicism and the Protestant movement](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

III. Non-Catholics are recognised to the contributions that they make to Christian belief overall [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Further reforms have been enacted with regard to the nature of the Catholic Church on the world stage, for instance the 1965 union with the Patriarch of Constantinople, whereby the 1054 schism was undermined. In addition to this, [Michael Ramsay](/wiki/Michael_Ramsey "Michael Ramsey"), the then Archbishop of Canterbury, received an [episcopal ring](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_ring "Ecclesiastical ring") in 1966; a mark of union which had not been seen since prior to the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"). Moreover, the Anglican, Roman Catholic International Committee was additionally established as a means of promoting communication and cohesion between the two denominations. This has since marked a new level of participation of the Catholic Faith in the aforementioned ecumenical movement, and therefore is the basis for increased participation from the faith, in the WCC.

### Special Commission on Orthodox Participation in the WCC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Special Commission on Orthodox Participation in the WCC")]

A *Special Commission* was set up by the eighth Harare Assembly in December 1998 to address Orthodox concerns about WCC membership and the council's decision-making style, public statements, worship practices, and other issues. It issued its final report in 2006.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Specific issues that it clarified were that the WCC does not formulate doctrine, does not have authority to rule on moral issues, nor does it have any ecclesiastical authority. Such authority is entirely internal to each individual member church. It proposed that the WCC adopt a [consensus](/wiki/Consensus_decision-making "Consensus decision-making") method of decision making. It proposed that Orthodox members be brought in parity with non-Orthodox members. It further proposed clarification that inter-confessional prayer at WCC events is not worship, particularly "it should avoid giving the impression of being the worship of a church", and confessional and inter-confessional prayer each be specifically identified as such at WCC events.

## Peace journalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Peace journalism")]

The WCC is also a prominent supporter and practitioning body for [Peace journalism](/wiki/Peace_journalism "Peace journalism"): journalism practice that aims to avoid a value bias in favor of violence that often characterizes coverage of conflict.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Spin-offs and related organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Spin-offs and related organizations")]

The [ACT Alliance](/wiki/ACT_Alliance "ACT Alliance"), bringing together over 100 church-backed relief and development organizations worldwide, was born out of the merger of ACT International (Action by Churches Together International) and ACT Development ([Action by Churches Together for Development](/w/index.php?title=Action_by_Churches_Together_for_Development&action=edit&redlink=1 "Action by Churches Together for Development (page does not exist)")) in March 2010. Both [ACT International](/wiki/ACT_International "ACT International"), established in 1995, and [ACT Development](/wiki/ACT_Development "ACT Development") (2007) were created through the leadership of the World Council of Churches (WCC). The two bodies coordinated the work of agencies related to the member churches of the WCC and the Lutheran World Federation in the areas of humanitarian emergencies and [poverty reduction](/wiki/Poverty_reduction "Poverty reduction") respectively.[[57]](#cite_note-oikoumene-57)

The [Ecumenical Advocacy Alliance](/wiki/Ecumenical_Advocacy_Alliance "Ecumenical Advocacy Alliance") was officially founded in December 2000 at a meeting convened by the WCC. There are currently 73 churches and Christian organizations that are members of the Alliance, from Catholic, Evangelical, Orthodox and Protestant traditions. These members, representing a combined constituency of tens of millions of people around the world, are committed to working together in public witness and action for justice on defined issues of common concern. Current campaigns are on Food and on HIV and AIDS.[[58]](#cite_note-oikoumene2-58)

The Ecumenical Church Loan Fund (ECLOF) was founded in 1946 as one of the world's first international micro-credit institutions in the service of the poor. [Willem Visser 't Hooft](/wiki/Willem_Visser_%27t_Hooft "Willem Visser 't Hooft"), then general secretary of the "WCC in process of formation" played an important role in founding ECLOF. It was he who sketched the prospects and challenges for the proposed institution and gave specific ideas on potential sources of funds. His inspiration and teamwork marked the beginning of a long and fruitful cooperation between ECLOF and the WCC.[[59]](#cite_note-eclof-59)

The Ecumenical Development Cooperative Society U.A (now known as [Oikocredit](/wiki/Oikocredit "Oikocredit")) was developed from discussions at the 1968 Uppsala 4th Assembly, regarding church divestment from financial institutions supporting apartheid-era South Africa and the war in Vietnam. After several years of planning, the cooperative society was founded in 1975 in the Netherlands to provide an alternative ethical investment vehicle to church institutions, by providing credit to productive enterprises serving economically disadvantaged populations. Originally organized for large institutional members of the WCC, by 1976 local congregations developed Support Associations to enable congregations as well as individuals to participate. EDCS became independent from the WCC in 1977.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

[Ecumenical News International](/wiki/Ecumenical_News_International "Ecumenical News International") (ENI) was launched in 1994 as a global news service reporting on ecumenical developments and other news of the Christian churches, and giving religious perspectives on news developments worldwide. The joint sponsors of ENI, which was based at the Ecumenical Centre in Geneva, Switzerland, were the World Council of Churches, the Lutheran World Federation, the World Alliance of Reformed Churches, and the Conference of European Churches, which also had their headquarters at the Ecumenical Centre.[[61]](#cite_note-61) A shortage of funds led to the suspension of the work of ENI in 2012.[[62]](#cite_note-christianpost-62) As of 2024 ENI remains closed.

### Alleged neglect of suffering church in Eastern Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Alleged neglect of suffering church in Eastern Europe")]

Some historians, the U.S. State Department and former KGB officers themselves have alleged and provided corroborating evidence that the KGB's influence directly, or through lobbying by means of a front organization, the Christian Peace Conference, resulted in the WCC's failure to recognize or act on calls for help from persecuted East European Christians at the 1983 Vancouver General Assembly.[[70]](#cite_note-active-70)[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71): 647–8

### Claims of infiltration and influence by the KGB

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Claims of infiltration and influence by the KGB")]

It is claimed the [KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB") has infiltrated and influenced past WCC councils and policy.[[19]](#cite_note-andrew-19) In 1992, Father [Gleb Yakunin](/wiki/Gleb_Yakunin "Gleb Yakunin"), a vice Chairman of a Russian parliamentary commission that investigated the activities of the KGB, citing verbatim KGB reports, claimed that its [Fifth Directorate](/wiki/KGB#Directorates "KGB") was actively involved in influencing WCC policy from 1967 to 1989.[[70]](#cite_note-active-70)[[72]](#cite_note-gleb-72) For example, in the 1983 WCC General Assembly in Vancouver, one cited document described the presence and activities of 47 KGB agents to secure the election of an "acceptable" candidate as General Secretary.[[72]](#cite_note-gleb-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) The [Mitrokhin Archive](/wiki/Mitrokhin_Archive#Penetration_of_churches "Mitrokhin Archive") reveals more about the depth of the penetration and influence wielded by the KGB over the WCC.[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71) [Metropolitan Nikodim](/wiki/Nikodim_Rotov "Nikodim Rotov") was a KGB agent, codenamed SVYATOSLAV, who served as one of six WCC Presidents from 1975 until his death.[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71): 729[[74]](#cite_note-besier-74) His earlier intervention had resulted in the WCC making no comment on the invasion of Czechoslovakia.[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71): 636 As a result of his influence and that of other agents, it is claimed the USSR was rarely publicly criticised.[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71): 637 In 1989, copies of the KGB documents claim "the WCC executive and central committee adopted public statements (eight) and messages (three)" which corresponded to its own political direction.[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71): 637 Appeals from suffering dissidents both from within the Russian Orthodox Church and Protestants were ignored in 1983.[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71): 647–8 Metropolitan Aleksi Ridiger of Tallinn and Estonia was repeatedly alleged to be a KGB agent codenamed DROZDOV, who in 1988 was awarded an honorary citation for services to the KGB by its chairman.[[71]](#cite_note-mitrokhin-71): 650[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-CWNrep-76) Despite official disavowals, [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") described the evidence as "compelling".[[77]](#cite_note-77) In 1990 he became [Alexius II](/wiki/Patriarch_Alexy_II_of_Moscow "Patriarch Alexy II of Moscow"), the 15th Patriarch of the Russian Orthodox Church. Upon his death in 2008, the WCC's official tribute, by its Council officers, described him as "courageous", "supportive and constructive" and the recipient of "abundant blessing", no reference was made to the allegations.[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)

### Attitude towards Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Attitude towards Israel")]

The World Council of Churches has been described as taking an adversarial position toward the state of Israel.[[80]](#cite_note-merkley-80) It has also been claimed the council has focused particularly on activities and publications criticizing Israel in comparison with other human rights issues.[[81]](#cite_note-vermaat-81)[[82]](#cite_note-Rottenberg-82) It is similarly claimed that it downplayed appeals from Egyptian Copts about human rights abuses under Sadat and Mubarak, in order to focus on its neighbour.[[80]](#cite_note-merkley-80) In 2009, the Council called for an international [boycott](/wiki/Boycott,_Divestment_and_Sanctions "Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions") on goods produced in Israeli settlements, which it described as 'illegal, unjust' and 'incompatible with peace'.[[83]](#cite_note-BDS-83) In 2013, the General Secretary was reported to claim in Cairo, "We support the Palestinians. The WCC supports the Palestinians, because they are in the right."[[84]](#cite_note-84) The WCC's [Ecumenical Accompaniment Programme in Palestine and Israel](/wiki/Ecumenical_Accompaniment_Programme_in_Palestine_and_Israel "Ecumenical Accompaniment Programme in Palestine and Israel") (EAPPI) has been criticised by the [Board of Deputies of British Jews](/wiki/Board_of_Deputies_of_British_Jews "Board of Deputies of British Jews") for promoting "an inflammatory and partisan programme at the expense of its interfaith relations".[[85]](#cite_note-JChron-85) The WCC secretariat was involved in preparing and helped disseminate the  [Kairos Palestine Document](/wiki/Kairos_Palestine "Kairos Palestine"), which declares "the Israeli occupation of Palestinian land is a sin against God and humanity because it deprives the Palestinians of their basic human rights", and in the view of one critic, its "authors want to see a single state".[[86]](#cite_note-lowe-86)
On the other hand, the WCC claims "Antisemitism is sin against God and man".[[87]](#cite_note-oikoumene3-87)

### Opposition to Christian Zionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=World_Council_of_Churches&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Opposition to Christian Zionism")]

[Christian Zionism](/wiki/Christian_Zionism "Christian Zionism"), which has long represented a major thread of [historic and contemporary Protestants](/wiki/Christian_Zionism#Protestant_Reformation "Christian Zionism"),[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-lewis-89) is characterised as a view which "distort(s) the interpretation of the Word of God" and "damage(s) intra-Christian relations".[[90]](#cite_note-mnd2-90)

> In this context, what is a source of concern is that Islamic fundamentalisms are giving rise to a counter reaction of other religious fundamentalisms, the most dangerous of which is Jewish fundamentalism which exploits the Islamic fundamentalist phenomenon to justify before western societies the distasteful aberrations of Zionism in Palestine.

— WCC working paper, Lebanon, May 2013[[91]](#cite_note-mnd-91)

Frank Chikane, moderator of the Commission of the Churches on International Affairs (CCIA) of the World Council of Churches (WCC), was criticised for using the term 'demons' to describe advocacy for Zionism in 2021.[[92]](#cite_note-alg-92)

On January 4, 2023, World Council of Churches general secretary [Jerry Pillay](/wiki/Jerry_Pillay "Jerry Pillay") joined the Episcopal Diocese of Jerusalem and all the churches of the Holy Land in condemning the desecration of the historic Protestant cemetery on Mount Zion.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

"From the beginning of the twentieth century the Ecumenical Patriarchate has shown a special concern for Christian reconciliation. At his accession in 1902, Patriarch Joachim III sent an encyclical letter to all the autocephalous Eastern Orthodox Churches, asking in particular for their opinion on relations with other Christian bodies. In January 1920 the Ecumenical Patriarchate followed this up with a bold and prophetic letter addressed 'To all the Churches of Christ, wherever they may be', urging closer co-operation among separated Christians, and suggesting a 'League of Churches', parallel to the newly founded League of Nations. Many of the ideas in this letter anticipate subsequent developments in the WCC. Constantinople, along with several of the other Eastern Orthodox Churches, was represented at the Faith and Order Conferences at Lausanne in 1927 and at Edinburgh in 1937. The Ecumenical Patriarchate also participated in the first Assembly of the WCC at Amsterdam in 1948, and has been a consistent supporter of the work of the WCC ever since."
